module.m1.ex1.exp: Selects every column from the students table. The asterisk (*) means 'all columns'.
module.m1.ex2.exp: Selects only the name column, returning just the data you actually need.
module.m2.ex1.exp: WHERE filters rows — only customers whose city is 'Istanbul' are returned.
module.m2.ex2.exp: ORDER BY sorts results alphabetically in reverse; LIMIT 2 keeps only the top two rows.
module.m3.ex1.exp: INSERT adds a new row to products with id = 4 and name = 'Mouse'.
module.m3.ex3.exp: UPDATE changes the name column to 'Laptop Pro' for the row where id = 1. Always use WHERE or all rows will be updated.
module.m3.ex2.exp: DELETE removes the row where id = 2. Always use WHERE — without it, all rows are deleted.
module.m7.ex3.exp: EXISTS returns true if the subquery finds at least one matching row. Here it returns customers who have at least one order.
module.m12.ex3.exp: RANK() assigns ranks like sports standings — if two rows tie, they get the same rank and the next rank has a gap (1,1,3).
module.m4.ex1.exp: CREATE TABLE defines a new table with two columns: an integer primary key and a text field.
module.m4.ex2.exp: DROP TABLE permanently deletes the logs table and all its data. This cannot be undone.
module.m5.ex1.exp: INNER JOIN returns only rows where both tables have a matching customer_id.
module.m5.ex2.exp: LEFT JOIN returns all customers, including those with no orders — missing order columns show NULL.
module.m6.ex1.exp: GROUP BY collapses rows sharing the same customer_id; SUM totals each group's orders.
module.m6.ex2.exp: HAVING filters groups after aggregation — here keeping only customers whose total exceeds 100.
module.m7.ex1.exp: The inner query finds customer IDs who placed orders; the outer query fetches their names.
module.m7.ex2.exp: Returns the name of the single customer with the highest order total using a scalar subquery.
module.m8.ex1.exp: UPDATE modifies an existing row — here it decrements the stock count by 1 for item id = 1.
module.m8.ex2.exp: SELECT after an update lets you verify the change; ORDER BY stock DESC shows highest stock first.
module.m9.ex1.exp: CASE WHEN acts like an if/else — assigns grade 'A', 'B', or 'C' based on each student's score.
module.m9.ex2.exp: CASE WHEN labels each inventory item 'In Stock' or 'Low Stock' based on the stock quantity.
module.m10.ex1.exp: UNION combines results from two queries and removes duplicate names automatically.
module.m10.ex2.exp: UNION ALL combines results without removing duplicates — faster and preserves all rows.
module.m11.ex1.exp: The CTE 'high_earners' is defined once with WITH, then queried like a regular table.
module.m11.ex2.exp: CTEs can include aggregations — 'totals' sums orders per customer, then filters for large totals.
module.m12.ex1.exp: ROW_NUMBER() assigns a rank to each student ordered by score, without collapsing any rows.
module.m12.ex2.exp: AVG() OVER () computes the overall average salary across all employees alongside each row.

challenge.fail: Result does not match challenge rules yet.
challenge.error: Query failed. Check SQL syntax and rules.

badge.firstTutorLesson.title: First Lesson
badge.firstTutorLesson.desc: Complete your first AI Coach lesson.
badge.firstExam.title: Exam Taker
tutor.title: SQL Coach
tutor.askProfession: Before we start, what do you do or which kind of work interests you?
tutor.askInterestWithProfessionTemplate: Profession saved as %1$@. Which lesson do you want to continue with?
tutor.welcome: Hi, I am your SQL coach. We are now in
tutor.askInterest: Which area are you interested in?
tutor.askGoalTemplate: Great. You picked %1$@. What exact result do you want from SQL?
tutor.goal.learning: I want to learn by examples
tutor.proposeCommandTemplate:
Perfect. I teach the same core command to everyone, adapted to your context:

%1$@

If this works, write Approve. If not, write Adjust and I will revise it.
tutor.revisedCommandTemplate:
Updated version based on your answer:

%1$@

If this is good, write Approve.
tutor.approvedTemplate:
Approved. This command is now your base pattern:

%1$@

Next step: solve an example or run it in Practice tab.
tutor.completedHint: We can now continue with examples and mini scenarios.
tutor.rotateSuggestion: Rotate your device for a better experience
onboarding.title1: SQL from zero — no prior experience needed
onboarding.desc1: Start with SELECT, work your way up to JOINs, CTEs, and Window Functions — 12 bite-sized modules.
onboarding.title2: Write real SQL, get instant feedback
onboarding.desc2: Every module ends with a hands-on challenge. Type SQL, hit Run, and see results right away.
onboarding.title3: AI Coach adapts to your interests
onboarding.desc3: Pick a real-world scenario — e-commerce, HR, analytics — and the AI guides you through it with examples and Q&A.
onboarding.desc4: Start at SELECT → Filter & Sort → Modify Data → Schema → JOINs → GROUP BY → Subqueries → CASE WHEN → UNION → CTEs → Window Functions.
onboarding.nameDescription: Add the name you want to see across your learning workspace.
tutor.package.analytics.title: SQL Foundations
tutor.package.analytics.desc: Define SQL, write core queries, and compare SQL dialects.
tutor.package.dml.title: Data Manipulation
tutor.package.dml.desc: Create tables, insert, update, delete rows, and alter schemas.
tutor.interest.dml.createTable.title: CREATE TABLE
tutor.interest.dml.createTable.desc: Learn to create new tables with columns and constraints.
tutor.interest.dml.insert.title: INSERT INTO
tutor.interest.dml.insert.desc: Add new rows to a table.
tutor.interest.dml.update.title: UPDATE SET
tutor.interest.dml.update.desc: Modify existing data in a table.
tutor.interest.dml.delete.title: DELETE FROM
tutor.interest.dml.alter.title: ALTER TABLE
tutor.interest.dml.alter.desc: Modify the structure of an existing table.
tutor.package.intermediate.title: Intermediate SQL
tutor.package.intermediate.desc: Build stronger query design with JOIN, GROUP BY and HAVING.
tutor.package.senior.title: Senior SQL
tutor.package.senior.desc: Advance with subqueries, CTEs and window functions.
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.definition.title: Define SQL and relational databases
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.definition.desc: Define SQL and explain how relational databases organize and store structured data.
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.schema.title: Tables, fields, records and data types
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.schema.desc: Identify tables, fields, records and data types in a database schema.
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.queries.title: Retrieve data with basic queries
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.queries.desc: Write basic SQL queries to select and retrieve data from relational tables.
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.clauses.title: Apply SELECT, FROM, DISTINCT and AS
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.clauses.desc: Use SELECT, FROM, DISTINCT and AS clauses to customize query outputs.
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.variants.title: Compare SQL variants and syntax
tutor.interest.sqlIntro.variants.desc: Compare SQL variants and understand syntax differences across common database systems.
tutor.interest.intermediate.join.title: JOIN fundamentals
tutor.interest.intermediate.join.desc: Combine related tables and aggregate meaningful metrics.
tutor.interest.intermediate.group.title: GROUP BY and HAVING
tutor.interest.intermediate.group.desc: Summarize data by groups and filter grouped outcomes.
tutor.interest.intermediate.aggregate.de: Design repeatable SUM/COUNT patterns for analytical reports.
tutor.interest.senior.subquery.title: Subquery design
tutor.interest.senior.subquery.desc: Use subqueries to compare rows with derived benchmarks.
tutor.interest.senior.cte.title: CTE workflows
tutor.interest.senior.cte.desc: Break complex logic into readable WITH steps.
tutor.interest.senior.window.title: Window functions
tutor.interest.senior.window.desc: Rank and compare rows with analytical window functions.
module.m1.title: SELECT Basics
module.m1.desc: Read data from tables with SELECT.
module.m1.body: SELECT reads data from a table. The basic structure is: SELECT columns FROM table_name. Use * to get all columns, or list column names separated by commas to get specific ones. The FROM keyword tells SQL which table to read from. Every SELECT query must have a FROM.
module.m1.obj1: Use SELECT * to read all rows and columns.
module.m1.obj2: List specific column names to select only what you need.
module.m1.quiz1.exp: SELECT is the SQL keyword for reading data.
module.m1.quiz2.prompt: Which statement correctly selects only name and email columns?
module.m1.quiz2.exp: Multiple columns are selected by separating them with commas.
module.m1.quiz3.prompt: What does the * symbol mean in SELECT * FROM table?
module.m1.quiz3.b: It selects all columns from the table.
module.m1.quiz3.exp: The asterisk (*) is a wildcard that means all columns.
module.m1.quiz4.prompt: Which query selects only the name column from the products table?
module.m1.quiz4.exp: Use SELECT column_name FROM table_name to pick specific columns.
module.m1.quiz5.prompt: What does the FROM keyword specify in a SELECT statement?
module.m1.quiz5.b: The number of rows to return.
module.m1.quiz5.c: Which table to read data from.
module.m1.quiz5.exp: FROM tells SQL which table contains the data you want to read.
module.m1.challenge1.title: List all students
module.m1.challenge1.prompt: Return id and name columns from the students table.
module.m1.challenge1.hint: Use SELECT id, name FROM students;
module.m1.challenge2.title: All product columns
module.m1.challenge2.prompt: Return all columns from the products table.
module.m1.challenge3.title: Employee name and department
module.m1.challenge3.prompt: Select only the name and department columns from the employees table.
module.m1.challenge3.hint: Use SELECT name, department FROM employees;
module.m2.title: Filtering and Sorting
module.m2.desc: Use WHERE, ORDER BY and LIMIT.
module.m2.body: WHERE filters rows, ORDER BY sorts results, LIMIT restricts row count.
module.m2.quiz1.exp: WHERE controls which rows stay in the result.
module.m2.quiz2.prompt: Which filter returns active customers in Istanbul?
module.m2.quiz2.exp: Use AND when both conditions must be true.
module.m2.quiz3.prompt: Which query returns the top 5 highest score rows?
module.m2.quiz3.a: SELECT * FROM scores LIMIT 5 ORDER BY score DESC;
module.m2.quiz3.b: SELECT * FROM scores ORDER score DESC LIMIT 5;
module.m2.quiz3.c: SELECT * FROM scores ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 5;
module.m2.quiz3.exp: Sort with ORDER BY, then trim with LIMIT.
module.m2.quiz4.prompt: Which query uses WHERE and ORDER BY in the correct order?
module.m2.quiz4.a: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE city = 'Ankara' ORDER BY name;
module.m2.quiz4.b: SELECT name FROM customers ORDER BY name WHERE city = 'Ankara';
module.m2.quiz4.c: SELECT name FROM customers ORDER city = 'Ankara' BY name;
module.m2.quiz4.exp: WHERE filters first, ORDER BY sorts the filtered rows.
module.m2.quiz5.prompt: Which query returns the first 5 Istanbul customers sorted by name ascending?
module.m2.quiz5.a: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE city = 'Istanbul' LIMIT 5 ORDER BY name ASC;
module.m2.quiz5.b: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE city = 'Istanbul' ORDER BY name ASC LIMIT 5;
module.m2.quiz5.c: SELECT TOP 5 name FROM customers WHERE city = 'Istanbul' ORDER BY name ASC;
module.m2.quiz5.exp: SQLite expects ORDER BY before LIMIT.
module.m2.challenge1.title: Istanbul customers
module.m2.challenge1.prompt: Return only names of customers living in Istanbul, sorted by name.
module.m2.challenge1.hint: Filter by city and order by name.
module.m2.challenge2.title: Expensive products
module.m2.challenge2.prompt: Return name and price of products costing more than 50, sorted by price descending.
module.m2.challenge2.hint: Use WHERE price > 50 and ORDER BY price DESC.
module.m2.challenge3.title: Top 3 scores
module.m2.challenge3.prompt: Return the name and score of the top 3 highest scoring students.
module.m2.challenge3.hint: Sort by score descending, then use LIMIT 3.
module.m1.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the SELECT query:
module.m1.dragfill1.exp: SELECT specifies columns, FROM specifies the table, WHERE filters rows.
module.m2.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the filter and sort query:
module.m2.dragfill1.exp: WHERE filters by age, ORDER BY sorts results, LIMIT restricts the row count.
module.m3.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the INSERT query:
module.m3.dragfill1.exp: INSERT INTO specifies the table; VALUES provides the data to insert.
module.m4.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the CREATE TABLE query:
module.m4.dragfill1.exp: CREATE TABLE creates a new table; you define column name and type.
module.m5.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the JOIN query:
module.m5.dragfill1.exp: INNER JOIN returns rows that match in both tables.
module.m6.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the GROUP BY / HAVING query:
module.m6.dragfill1.exp: AVG computes averages; GROUP BY groups results; HAVING filters groups.
module.m7.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the subquery:
module.m7.dragfill1.exp: Subqueries generate a comparison value used by the outer query.
module.m8.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the combined query:
module.m8.dragfill1.exp: LEFT JOIN links customers and orders; DESC sorts in descending order.
module.m9.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the CASE WHEN query:
module.m9.dragfill1.exp: CASE returns conditional values; ELSE handles the default case.
module.m10.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the UNION query:
module.m10.dragfill1.exp: UNION combines two query results removing duplicates; ORDER BY sorts the output.
module.m11.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the CTE (WITH) query:
module.m11.dragfill1.exp: WITH defines a named temporary query (CTE) that is referenced below by the same name.
module.m12.dragfill1.prompt: Fill in the blanks in the window function query:
module.m12.dragfill1.exp: RANK() is a window function; OVER with PARTITION BY ranks within groups.
module.m3.title: Insert, Update, Delete
module.m3.body: INSERT adds rows, UPDATE changes rows, DELETE removes rows.
module.m3.quiz1.exp: DELETE removes selected rows from a table.
module.m3.quiz2.prompt: Which command correctly inserts a new product row?
module.m3.quiz2.exp: Use INSERT INTO ... VALUES to add a new row.
module.m3.quiz3.prompt: Which query updates the name to Mouse for id = 2?
module.m3.quiz3.b: UPDATE products SET name = 'Mouse' WHERE id = 2;
module.m3.quiz3.c: MODIFY products SET name = 'Mouse' WHERE id = 2;
module.m3.quiz3.exp: Correct syntax is UPDATE table SET column = value WHERE condition.
module.m3.quiz4.prompt: What happens when DELETE is used without WHERE?
module.m3.quiz4.a: Only the first row is deleted.
module.m3.quiz4.c: All rows in the table are deleted.
module.m3.quiz4.exp: DELETE FROM table; removes every row.
module.m3.quiz5.prompt: Which script correctly increases stock by 5 for id=2, then reads the row?
module.m3.quiz5.a: SELECT * FROM inventory WHERE id = 2; UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock + 5 WHERE id = 2;
module.m3.quiz5.b: UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock + 5 WHERE id = 2; SELECT id, stock FROM inventory WHERE id = 2;
module.m3.quiz5.c: INSERT INTO inventory SET stock = stock + 5 WHERE id = 2; SELECT * FROM inventory;
module.m3.quiz5.exp: Update first, then query to verify the new value.
module.m3.challenge1.title: Delete one product
module.m3.challenge1.prompt: Delete the product with id 3 so table has 2 rows left.
module.m3.challenge1.hint: Use DELETE FROM products WHERE id = 3.
module.m4.title: Schema Operations
module.m4.obj1: Create a table with column definitions.
module.m4.obj2: Add columns to an existing table with ALTER TABLE.
module.m4.ex3.exp: ALTER TABLE adds a new column to an existing table. Here we add a 'level' column with a default value of 'info'.
module.m4.quiz1.prompt: Which command creates a new table?
module.m4.quiz1.exp: CREATE TABLE is the standard SQL command.
module.m4.quiz2.prompt: Which data type is best for whole numbers only?
module.m4.quiz2.exp: INTEGER is used for whole-number values.
module.m4.quiz3.prompt: Which command correctly adds an email column to an existing table?
module.m4.quiz3.a: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email TEXT;
module.m4.quiz3.b: UPDATE TABLE users ADD email TEXT;
module.m4.quiz3.c: CREATE COLUMN email TEXT ON users;
module.m4.quiz3.exp: Use ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN to add a new column.
module.m4.quiz4.prompt: Which statement drops a table only if it exists?
module.m4.quiz4.exp: Correct syntax is DROP TABLE IF EXISTS table_name.
module.m4.quiz5.prompt: Which definition creates id as primary key and name as non-null?
module.m4.quiz5.a: CREATE TABLE categories (id INTEGER, name TEXT NOT NULL, PRIMARY id);
module.m4.quiz5.b: CREATE TABLE categories (id INTEGER KEY, name TEXT REQUIRED);
module.m4.quiz5.c: CREATE TABLE categories (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL);
module.m4.quiz5.exp: PRIMARY KEY and NOT NULL must be declared with valid SQL column syntax.
module.m4.challenge1.title: Create categories table
module.m4.challenge1.prompt: Create a table named categories with id and name columns.
module.m5.title: Join Basics
module.m5.body: JOIN combines rows from two tables based on a shared key. The ON clause defines how the tables are related. INNER JOIN returns only rows with a match in both tables. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table — when there is no match in the right table the missing values appear as NULL. Table aliases like c and o keep queries shorter.
module.m5.obj1: Use JOIN with ON to link two tables by a shared key.
module.m5.obj2: Understand the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN.
module.m5.quiz1.prompt: Which keyword combines rows from two tables?
module.m5.quiz1.exp: JOIN is the SQL operator for combining tables.
module.m5.quiz2.prompt: Which statement correctly connects customers and orders by customer id?
module.m5.quiz2.b: SELECT * FROM customers ON customers.id = orders.customer_id;
module.m5.quiz2.c: SELECT * FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id;
module.m5.quiz2.exp: JOIN needs an ON condition to define the relationship key.
module.m5.quiz3.prompt: Which JOIN keeps customers even when they have no orders?
module.m5.quiz3.exp: LEFT JOIN preserves all rows from the left table.
module.m5.quiz4.prompt: What does the ON clause specify in a JOIN?
module.m5.quiz4.a: Which columns to display in the result.
module.m5.quiz4.b: How the two tables are related.
module.m5.quiz4.exp: ON defines the matching condition between the two joined tables.
module.m5.quiz5.prompt: In a LEFT JOIN, what happens when a customer has no matching orders?
module.m5.quiz5.a: The customer row is excluded from results.
module.m5.quiz5.c: The customer row appears with NULL values for order columns.
module.m5.quiz5.exp: LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table; missing matches become NULL.
module.m5.challenge1.title: Customer totals
module.m5.challenge1.prompt: Return customer name and order total using JOIN, ordered by customer name.
module.m5.challenge1.hint: Join customers and orders on customer id.
module.m6.title: GROUP BY and HAVING
module.m6.body: GROUP BY builds groups and aggregate functions summarize each group.
module.m6.quiz1.exp: HAVING is evaluated after GROUP BY.
module.m6.quiz2.prompt: Which query start correctly computes average price per category?
module.m6.quiz2.a: SELECT category, AVG(price) FROM products GROUP BY category;
module.m6.quiz2.b: SELECT category, AVG(price) FROM products HAVING category;
module.m6.quiz2.c: SELECT AVG(price) category FROM products GROUP category;
module.m6.quiz2.exp: Category-level summaries require GROUP BY category.
module.m6.quiz3.prompt: Which clause filters grouped rows when SUM(total) must be greater than 500?
module.m6.quiz3.exp: Aggregate predicates belong in HAVING, not WHERE.
module.m6.quiz4.prompt: Which query uses an aggregate alias with HAVING correctly?
module.m6.quiz4.a: SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) total_sum FROM orders HAVING total_sum > 100 GROUP BY customer_id;
module.m6.quiz4.b: SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) AS total_sum FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id WHERE total_sum > 100;
module.m6.quiz4.c: SELECT customer_id, SUM(total) AS total_sum FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING total_sum > 100;
module.m6.quiz4.exp: GROUP BY first, then HAVING for grouped filtering.
module.m6.quiz5.prompt: Which query returns the category with the highest average price?
module.m6.quiz5.a: SELECT category, AVG(price) AS avg_price FROM products GROUP BY category ORDER BY avg_price DESC LIMIT 1;
module.m6.quiz5.b: SELECT category, AVG(price) AS avg_price FROM products ORDER BY avg_price DESC GROUP BY category LIMIT 1;
module.m6.quiz5.c: SELECT TOP 1 category, AVG(price) FROM products GROUP BY category ORDER BY AVG(price) DESC;
module.m6.quiz5.exp: Correct order is GROUP BY, then ORDER BY, then LIMIT in SQLite.
module.m6.challenge1.title: High value customers
module.m6.challenge1.prompt: Return customer_id and SUM(total) where total sum is over 100.
module.m6.challenge1.hint: Use GROUP BY customer_id and HAVING total_sum > 100.
module.m7.title: Subqueries
module.m7.obj1: Use IN with a subquery result set.
module.m7.obj2: Use scalar subquery for single value filters.
module.m7.quiz1.a: A query nested inside another query.
module.m7.quiz1.b: A query that only updates rows.
module.m7.quiz1.c: A query with no FROM clause.
module.m7.quiz1.exp: Subqueries can appear in WHERE, FROM, and SELECT contexts.
module.m7.quiz2.prompt: Which query correctly uses IN with a subquery to return customers with orders?
module.m7.quiz2.a: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE id IN orders.customer_id;
module.m7.quiz2.b: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders);
module.m7.quiz2.c: SELECT name FROM customers IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders);
module.m7.quiz2.exp: IN must compare against a parenthesized subquery result.
module.m7.quiz3.prompt: Which query uses a scalar subquery to find the customer_id of the maximum order?
module.m7.quiz3.a: SELECT customer_id FROM orders WHERE total = (SELECT MAX(total) FROM orders);
module.m7.quiz3.b: SELECT customer_id FROM orders WHERE total = SELECT MAX(total) FROM orders;
module.m7.quiz3.c: SELECT customer_id FROM orders WHERE total MAX(total);
module.m7.quiz3.exp: Scalar subqueries return one value and can be used in comparisons.
module.m7.quiz4.a: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE EXISTS orders;
module.m7.quiz4.b: SELECT name FROM customers EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.id);
module.m7.quiz4.c: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE orders.customer_id = customers.id);
module.m7.quiz4.exp: EXISTS is used in WHERE with a full subquery.
module.m7.quiz5.prompt: Which correlated subquery returns orders above each customer's own average?
module.m7.quiz5.a: SELECT * FROM orders o WHERE total > (SELECT AVG(total) FROM orders);
module.m7.quiz5.b: SELECT * FROM orders o WHERE o.total > (SELECT AVG(o2.total) FROM orders o2 WHERE o2.customer_id = o.customer_id);
module.m7.quiz5.c: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > AVG(total) GROUP BY customer_id;
module.m7.quiz5.exp: Correlated subqueries reference the outer query key (customer_id).
module.m7.challenge1.title: Top spender
module.m7.challenge1.prompt: Return the name of the customer with the highest single order total.
module.m7.challenge1.hint: Use a subquery with ORDER BY total DESC LIMIT 1.
module.m8.title: Mini Project Querying
module.m8.body: This module combines ordering and projection in a mini inventory scenario.
module.m8.obj1: Write readable result queries for business data.
module.m8.obj2: Order results to match requirement output.
module.m8.quiz1.prompt: Which clause sorts descending by stock?
module.m8.quiz1.exp: ORDER BY is required for sorting.
module.m8.quiz2.prompt: Which query filters products with stock > 10 and sorts by name?
module.m8.quiz2.a: SELECT name, stock FROM inventory WHERE stock > 10 ORDER BY name ASC;
module.m8.quiz2.b: SELECT name, stock FROM inventory ORDER BY name ASC WHERE stock > 10;
module.m8.quiz2.c: SELECT name, stock FROM inventory FILTER stock > 10 ORDER BY name;
module.m8.quiz2.exp: Apply WHERE first, then ORDER BY.
module.m8.quiz3.prompt: Which script decreases stock by 2 for id=1, then reads the new stock?
module.m8.quiz3.a: SELECT stock FROM inventory WHERE id = 1; UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock - 2 WHERE id = 1;
module.m8.quiz3.b: UPDATE inventory stock = stock - 2 WHERE id = 1; SELECT stock FROM inventory WHERE id = 1;
module.m8.quiz3.c: UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock - 2 WHERE id = 1; SELECT stock FROM inventory WHERE id = 1;
module.m8.quiz3.exp: UPDATE requires SET, then SELECT can verify the updated value.
module.m8.quiz4.prompt: Which script correctly restocks item id=3 by 10 units, then reads its new stock?
module.m8.quiz4.a: SELECT stock FROM inventory WHERE id = 3; UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock + 10 WHERE id = 3;
module.m8.quiz4.b: UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock + 10 WHERE id = 3; SELECT name, stock FROM inventory WHERE id = 3;
module.m8.quiz4.c: INSERT INTO inventory SET stock = stock + 10 WHERE id = 3; SELECT stock FROM inventory;
module.m8.quiz4.exp: Always UPDATE first, then SELECT to verify the new value.
module.m8.quiz5.prompt: Which query returns the 3 lowest-stock products for a stock alert report?
module.m8.quiz5.a: SELECT name, stock FROM inventory ORDER BY stock ASC LIMIT 3;
module.m8.quiz5.b: SELECT name, stock FROM inventory LIMIT 3 ORDER BY stock ASC;
module.m8.quiz5.c: SELECT TOP 3 name, stock FROM inventory ORDER BY stock ASC;
module.m8.quiz5.exp: Use ORDER BY stock ASC then LIMIT 3 in SQLite.
module.m8.challenge1.title: Inventory ranking
module.m8.challenge1.prompt: Return product name and stock ordered by stock descending.
module.m8.challenge1.hint: Select name, stock and add ORDER BY stock DESC.
module.m9.title: Conditional Logic (CASE WHEN)
module.m9.desc: Add if/else logic directly inside a SQL query.
module.m9.body: CASE WHEN lets you create new values based on conditions — like an if/else inside SELECT. Each WHEN checks a condition; the first match wins. ELSE provides the default if nothing matches. Without ELSE, unmatched rows return NULL.
module.m9.obj1: Write CASE WHEN expressions inside SELECT.
module.m9.obj2: Assign labels and categories to rows based on conditions.
module.m9.quiz1.prompt: What is the purpose of CASE WHEN in SQL?
module.m9.quiz1.b: To add conditional logic inside a SELECT query.
module.m9.quiz1.exp: CASE WHEN produces a derived value per row based on conditions — it does not filter rows.
module.m9.quiz2.prompt: Which expression correctly returns 'Pass' if score >= 60, else 'Fail'?
module.m9.quiz2.a: CASE WHEN score > 60 THEN 'Pass' ELSE 'Fail' END
module.m9.quiz2.c: CASE WHEN score >= 60 THEN 'Pass' ELSE 'Fail' END
module.m9.quiz2.exp: The correct syntax is CASE WHEN condition THEN value ... ELSE default END.
module.m9.quiz3.prompt: What does the ELSE clause do in a CASE WHEN expression?
module.m9.quiz3.a: Provides a default value when no WHEN condition matches.
module.m9.quiz3.b: Always runs first before other conditions.
module.m9.quiz3.c: Removes rows that don't match any condition.
module.m9.quiz3.exp: ELSE is the fallback — it runs only when every WHEN condition is false.
module.m9.quiz4.prompt: A CASE WHEN with no ELSE clause returns ____ when no condition matches?
module.m9.quiz4.exp: Without ELSE, SQL returns NULL for any unmatched row.
module.m9.quiz5.prompt: Which query correctly labels each order as 'Large' (total > 200) or 'Small'?
module.m9.quiz5.a: SELECT id, CASE total > 200 THEN 'Large' ELSE 'Small' END AS size FROM orders;
module.m9.quiz5.b: SELECT id, CASE WHEN total > 200 THEN 'Large' ELSE 'Small' END AS size FROM orders;
module.m9.quiz5.c: SELECT id, IF(total > 200, 'Large', 'Small') AS size FROM orders;
module.m9.quiz5.exp: SQLite uses CASE WHEN, not IF(). Always include WHEN before the condition.
module.m9.challenge1.title: Label products by price
module.m9.challenge1.prompt: Label each product as 'Expensive' if price > 100, otherwise 'Affordable'. Return name and label sorted by name.
module.m9.challenge1.hint: Use CASE WHEN price > 100 THEN 'Expensive' ELSE 'Affordable' END AS label.
module.m10.title: UNION & Set Operations
module.m10.desc: Combine results from multiple SELECT queries into one.
module.m10.body: UNION stacks the rows from two SELECT queries vertically. UNION removes duplicates; UNION ALL keeps every row including duplicates. Both queries must have the same number of columns and compatible types.
module.m10.obj1: Combine two SELECT results with UNION.
module.m10.obj2: Understand when to use UNION vs UNION ALL.
module.m10.quiz1.a: Combines two tables horizontally by adding columns.
module.m10.quiz1.b: Combines two SELECT results vertically by adding rows.
module.m10.quiz1.c: Updates rows in both tables at once.
module.m10.quiz1.exp: UNION stacks rows from two queries into one result set.
module.m10.quiz2.prompt: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
module.m10.quiz2.a: UNION keeps duplicates; UNION ALL removes them.
module.m10.quiz2.b: UNION removes duplicates; UNION ALL keeps them.
module.m10.quiz2.exp: UNION deduplicates; UNION ALL is faster because it skips that step.
module.m10.quiz3.prompt: When using UNION, both SELECT statements must have:
module.m10.quiz3.exp: UNION requires matching column counts and compatible data types.
module.m10.quiz4.prompt: Which query correctly returns all names from customers and employees without duplicates?
module.m10.quiz4.a: SELECT name FROM customers + SELECT name FROM employees;
module.m10.quiz4.c: SELECT name FROM customers UNION SELECT name FROM employees;
module.m10.quiz4.exp: UNION is the correct operator. The comma creates a cross join, not a union.
module.m10.quiz5.prompt: UNION ALL is faster than UNION because:
module.m10.quiz5.a: It skips the sorting and duplicate removal step.
module.m10.quiz5.b: It only returns the first result set.
module.m10.quiz5.exp: Removing duplicates requires sorting. UNION ALL skips this, making it faster.
module.m10.challenge1.title: Combine customer and supplier names
module.m10.challenge1.prompt: Return all unique names from both customers and suppliers combined, sorted alphabetically.
module.m10.challenge1.hint: Use UNION (not UNION ALL) to remove duplicates, then ORDER BY name.
module.m11.title: CTEs (WITH Clause)
module.m11.desc: Name and reuse subqueries for cleaner, readable SQL.
module.m11.body: A Common Table Expression (CTE) lets you name a subquery and reference it like a temporary table. Write WITH name AS (query), then use the name in the main SELECT below. CTEs make complex queries much easier to read and debug.
module.m11.obj1: Define a CTE using the WITH keyword.
module.m11.obj2: Reference the CTE in the main SELECT query.
module.m11.quiz1.exp: CTEs always start with WITH, followed by the CTE name and AS (...).
module.m11.quiz2.prompt: What is the main benefit of a CTE?
module.m11.quiz2.a: It always runs faster than a subquery.
module.m11.quiz2.b: It makes complex queries more readable by naming subqueries.
module.m11.quiz2.exp: CTEs are a readability tool — they name subqueries so the main query is easier to follow.
module.m11.quiz3.prompt: Which syntax correctly creates a CTE named top_scores?
module.m11.quiz3.a: CTE top_scores AS (SELECT * FROM scores WHERE score > 90)
module.m11.quiz3.b: WITH top_scores AS (SELECT * FROM scores WHERE score > 90)
module.m11.quiz3.c: WITH top_scores = (SELECT * FROM scores WHERE score > 90)
module.m11.quiz3.exp: Use WITH name AS (query). CTE is not a keyword and = is not valid here.
module.m11.quiz4.prompt: After defining a CTE, how do you use it?
module.m11.quiz4.a: Reference it by name in the SELECT that immediately follows.
module.m11.quiz4.c: CTEs are automatically applied to all queries in the session.
module.m11.quiz4.exp: The CTE only exists for the single SELECT statement that follows the WITH block.
module.m11.quiz5.prompt: Which query correctly uses a CTE to find orders above 100?
module.m11.quiz5.a: WITH big AS (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > 100) SELECT * FROM orders;
module.m11.quiz5.b: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > 100) AS big;
module.m11.quiz5.c: WITH big AS (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > 100) SELECT * FROM big;
module.m11.quiz5.exp: The main SELECT must reference the CTE name (big), not the original table.
module.m11.challenge1.title: Find large orders with a CTE
module.m11.challenge1.prompt: Use a CTE named big_orders to select orders where total > 100. Return customer_id and total, sorted by total descending.
module.m11.challenge1.hint: Write WITH big_orders AS (SELECT ... WHERE total > 100) then SELECT from big_orders.
module.m12.title: Window Functions
module.m12.desc: Rank, number, and compare rows without collapsing them.
module.m12.body: Window functions compute values across a set of related rows without collapsing them like GROUP BY does. Use function() OVER (ORDER BY ...) to define the window. Common functions: ROW_NUMBER() gives unique rank, RANK() allows ties, AVG() OVER () computes the overall average per row.
module.m12.quiz1.prompt: Which keyword defines a window for a window function?
module.m12.quiz1.exp: OVER() is what makes a function a window function. PARTITION BY is optional inside OVER.
module.m12.quiz2.prompt: Which function assigns a unique sequential number to each row based on sort order?
module.m12.quiz2.exp: ROW_NUMBER() gives a unique number to every row. RANK() can repeat numbers when rows tie.
module.m12.quiz3.prompt: What is the difference between ROW_NUMBER() and RANK()?
module.m12.quiz3.a: ROW_NUMBER gives unique numbers; RANK can have gaps when rows tie.
module.m12.quiz3.c: RANK only works inside GROUP BY.
module.m12.quiz3.exp: When two rows tie, RANK() assigns them the same number and skips the next (e.g., 1,1,3). ROW_NUMBER always produces 1,2,3.
module.m12.quiz4.prompt: Which query correctly ranks products by price from highest to lowest?
module.m12.quiz4.a: SELECT name, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY price DESC) AS rank FROM products;
module.m12.quiz4.b: SELECT name, ROW_NUMBER() OVER ORDER BY price DESC AS rank FROM products;
module.m12.quiz4.c: SELECT name, RANK(price DESC) AS rank FROM products;
module.m12.quiz4.exp: OVER must be followed by parentheses: OVER (ORDER BY ...).
module.m12.quiz5.b: The overall average salary across all rows, shown on every row.
module.m12.quiz5.c: The average of only the top 5 salaries.
module.m12.quiz5.exp: An empty OVER () applies the window to all rows, so every row gets the same overall average.
module.m12.challenge1.title: Rank students by score
module.m12.challenge1.prompt: Rank all students by score descending using ROW_NUMBER(). Return name and rank, sorted by rank.
module.m12.challenge1.hint: Use ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS rank, then ORDER BY rank.

module.m1.realworld: Data analysts at companies like Amazon run SELECT queries millions of times per day to pull reports from databases.
module.m1.outcome: After this module, you can retrieve any data from a database table.
module.m1.guided1.desc: Step 1: Start with SELECT * to get all columns from a table.
module.m1.guided2.desc: Step 2: Specify only the columns you need instead of *.
module.m1.guided3.desc: Step 3: Add ORDER BY to sort results — DESC means highest first.
module.m1.err1.prompt: Find the mistake in this SQL query:
module.m1.err1.a: SELEC and FORM are misspelled (should be SELECT and FROM)
module.m1.err1.b: The * is wrong — you must specify columns
module.m1.err1.c: The semicolon at the end is unnecessary
module.m1.err1.exp: SQL keywords like SELECT and FROM must be spelled exactly right. Even one typo causes an error.
module.m1.out1.a: The first 2 names from the students table
module.m1.out1.b: All names from the students table
module.m1.out1.c: The last 2 names from the students table
module.m1.out1.exp: LIMIT 2 restricts the result to only 2 rows, taken from the top of the result set.
module.m2.realworld: E-commerce platforms filter millions of orders by date, status, and customer ID using WHERE clauses every second.
module.m2.outcome: After this module, you can filter and sort any query result precisely.
module.misc.guidedSubtitle: Follow the steps to build a query
module.misc.miniCheckDone: Well done! Now for the main quiz.
career.data_analyst.title: Data Analyst
career.data_analyst.desc: Master querying, filtering, aggregation and reporting — the core skills every data analyst needs.
career.data_analyst.audience: Aspiring data analysts, Excel users transitioning to SQL
career.bi_analyst.title: BI Analyst
career.bi_analyst.desc: Build advanced analytical queries with CTEs, window functions and complex joins for business intelligence.
career.bi_analyst.audience: BI professionals, dashboard builders, reporting specialists
career.backend_dev.title: Backend Developer
career.backend_dev.desc: Learn to design schemas, write CRUD operations, and build efficient queries for production applications.
career.backend_dev.audience: Software developers, web developers, API builders
career.data_engineer.title: Data Engineer
career.data_engineer.desc: From schema design to data transformation — learn the SQL patterns that power data pipelines.
career.data_engineer.audience: ETL developers, data pipeline builders, infrastructure engineers
career.product_analyst.title: Product Analyst
career.product_analyst.desc: Analyze user behavior, build funnels, and measure product metrics with advanced SQL techniques.
career.product_analyst.audience: Product managers, growth analysts, startup founders
career.interview_prep.title: SQL Interview Prep
career.interview_prep.desc: Comprehensive preparation covering all major SQL topics asked in technical interviews.
career.interview_prep.audience: Job seekers, career changers, interview candidates

career.milestone.halfway.desc: You're making great progress. Keep going!
career.milestone.data_analyst_complete.d: You have all the SQL skills for a data analyst role.

career.milestone.advanced_analytics.desc: You can write complex analytical queries.
career.milestone.bi_complete.desc: You're equipped for business intelligence work.
career.milestone.crud_master.desc: You can create, read, update and delete data confidently.
You're equipped to build data pipelines.
career.milestone.product_analyst_complet: You can analyze product metrics with SQL.
career.milestone.interview_ready.desc: You're prepared for SQL technical interviews.
